chapters.title: Appreciate even the tiny positive moments
chapters.text:
Most of us go from one day to another, trying to surmount different obstacles. These obstacles can be overwhelming, so we can't notice the positive experiences. By default, our brains diminish the positives and exaggerate the negatives.

In this summary, you will learn why and how to tap into the power of positive experiences to transform the quality of your life. Good moments can help develop a confident and relaxed brain. These moments are not expensive to experience. They are everyday activities like having a cup of coffee, feeling satisfaction after doing a chore, or enjoying the kindness someone significant shows to you.

To be well, to cope with stress, and to succeed, we need inner strengths hidden in our brain structure.

Our ancestors tapped into their inner strength, but their primary objective for doing so was to escape danger. Consequently, humanity developed a negativity bias. This idea forces us to focus on bad experiences and ignore the good ones. The power of a positive background can satisfy all our needs, from the basic desire for safety to effective interaction with others. Eventually, we will find peace and well-being within ourselves that is independent of external conditions.

Building inner strength, contentment, and love enables you to stay unperturbed through life’s situations.

Your brain directs your thoughts, feelings, and actions. Positive experiences can affect your brain. Now, it is up to you to control the process and stop being a victim of circumstances.

Taking in the good helps you see the good in yourself, and in the world and other people. ~ Rick Hanson

chapters.title: Enhance your inner power with joyful experiences
chapters.text:
Everyone must cultivate seven inner strengths:

• Harmony
• Fulfillment
• Endurance
• Self-belief
• Drive
• Understanding
• Compassion

With this core, you can deal with life’s difficulties, such as healing from old pain and recovering from a stressful life.

The good news is that you can develop these strengths, even if you feel they are lacking. You must pay close attention to what’s happening in your mind. Locate the positive thoughts you have and expand them. For example, continue developing this idea if you suddenly think how wonderful it is to have good friends. Feel grateful for the good people around you, remember the beautiful moments you spent together, and smile. Think of your mind as a fertile, uncultivated field. If left untouched, weeds and unwanted plants will grow on it. However, if you want flowers in your area, you must plant them there intentionally.

As you plant and nurture positive thoughts, you begin to change the structure of your brain.

When mental activity takes place, you establish a neural connection. When you repeat a mental activity, it strengthens the neural link. This type of strengthening is called experience-dependent neuroplasticity.

When you seek to develop these inner strengths, the best way is to experience them. Think of a good mental state as software you must install into your brain.

To consolidate a positive experience and turn it into a stable neural structure, remember five factors:

1. Duration: The longer you experience a good event, the better you remember it. If you find your mind wandering, you can return it by recreating the stimulus for the experience.
2. Intensity: We remember vivid impressions for a long time, so try to make them unforgettable.
3. Multimodality: It means awareness of the different aspects of an experience. For example, when you think of a friend's good qualities, you can weave feelings like love or peace into it.
4. Novelty: Find something new in your positive experiences, especially if they are commonplace. Explore how your state changes when you hug a loved one.
5. Personal relevance: It includes analyzing how the excellent experience helped you and what it taught you.

Did you know? Forgetting some information is an entirely natural property of our brain because removing unnecessary details helps our nervous system to maintain its plasticity.

chapters.title: You don't have to survive today
chapters.text:
The modern brain is a product of 600 million years of evolution. You have the strategies for survival developed by ancient primates and humans in your brain. The problem is that most of these ancestors developed a particular alertness to a danger that enabled them to survive. Wild animals killed those who didn't improve this capacity. The genes of the survivors are what run through your body today. That’s why you tend to look for bad news and overreact. Your brain focuses on bad news or bad experiences. We store this experience in a neural structure that forms the baseline for our actions. Although you might be happy, you are addicted to a negative mindset that exposes you to stress, worry, frustration, and sadness.

Because of fear, we focus much more on the bad outcome of events than on the good ones.

Negative experiences train our brains to see problems but not opportunities everywhere. To switch, we must seek to experience and notice more joyful moments. Our current brain structure only saves positive experiences once we choose to install them.

Negativity bias may be helpful for survival, but it cannot help you thrive. A good life, healthy relationships, personal growth, and long, healthy living are possible only by storing the good in our neural networks.

In the responsive mode, your emotions are positive. You pursue your goals with enthusiasm and optimism, stand up to injustice, live with passion, make love, spend time with friends, cheer your children on, assert yourself, and are strong. You are in your essence — your natural home base.

The reverse is the case for the reactive mode. This mode is the red setting of your brain. You enter into an allostatic state where you suspend your long-term needs for urgent demands. The brain activates this state when you experience a threat, loss, or rejection.

By design, this red mode is necessary, although infrequent, brief, and moderate. However, modern life has violated this design. With the influx of information, multitasking, working long hours, and attending to many urgent tasks, there is hardly time for responsive recovery.

What is essential is not what conditions we are in now but how we perceive them. By focusing on the good, we can help ourselves change external circumstances.

chapters.title: Take care of your mental health to stay in responsive mode
chapters.text:
The operating systems represent the three stages of development of our brains. Our brain has constantly evolved from the brain stem to the subcortex and then to the cortex. Think of these stages as the floors of a 3-story building:

1. The brain stem supports the subcortex from the top of the spinal cord.
2. The cortex is responsible for abstract reasoning and represents the outer shell or superstructure of the brain. It deals with memory, ambition, empathy, cooperation, planning, and language.
3. The subcortex handles emotion, connection, and motivation.

We created the autonomic nervous system and the vagus nerve at the same time. The autonomic nervous system has two departments: parasympathetic and sympathetic, which complement each other. In a hazardous scenario, the sympathetic one supports pursuing opportunities and fighting or escaping, whereas the parasympathetic promotes retreat or freezing. The parasympathetic inhibits or calms while the sympathetic energizes or excites.

Positive thinking and pleasant memories will help you control your brain and its reactions.

In simple terms, we have two brain modes: green and red. The green brain is responsive, while the red brain is reactive. When we are in green mode, our brain works to build us up, while in the red one, we can't develop and grow. The ancients spent more time in green mode and triggered red mode only when the situation demanded it. Today, we are primarily in a red way of reacting because of modern life's stress and anxiety.

Let's imagine, for example, that your phone has run out of charge on the way to a friend, and you can't warn them that you will be late. If, before this, you had a stressful day at work, your boss yelled at you, and you didn't manage your emotions, then you will probably stop and sit on the bench upset or go home. However, if you are in a good mood and a calm emotional state at that moment, you will likely start looking for opportunities: ask a passerby to call or go to a nearby cafe to recharge your phone. Focusing on the positive moments of life is essential to get yourself back into green brain mode.

If you take care of the minutes, the years will take care of themselves. ~ Rick Hanson

chapters.title: Switch from negativity to responsivity
chapters.text:
The five essential features of negativity bias are:

• Reacting in the face of challenging situations.
• Having a sense of dissatisfaction, disconnection, and uneasiness even when things are fine.
• Over-learning from bad experiences.
• Becoming quick to switch to reactive mode.
• Being slow to return to responsive mode even when conditions have returned to normalcy.

To turn your brain to a responsivity bias, you need to:

• Rely on your repeated positive experiences. For example, if the cashier smiles at you every time in the store, you can perceive it as support. Consider this situation in detail, give thanks, and remember under challenging moments to sustain yourself.
• Be open to the good. Remind yourself that you were born to be healthy, happy, and prosperous.
• Make positive experiences overpower harmful ones by finding more ways to have them. For example, if you don't get a job the first time, keep going and try again to finally feel the joy of satisfying your needs.
• Try to look for the good in any situation. Although the employer did not hire you the first time, you still went to the interview. It is already a victory and a positive experience.
• Initiate your recovery from reactive mode by taking in the good.

Developing a responsivity bias does not mean you won’t get into reactive mode. Instead, it helps you return quickly to your center after the reactive experience has stretched you.

You can be in responsive and reactive modes simultaneously. You may worry about an upcoming exam, trying not to think about it (avoiding). At the same time, you can feel grateful to have an exciting job (approaching) and be glad to have a friend who always supports you (attaching). Slow breathing, relaxation, focusing on the good, and mentions of sincere support from loved ones will help you change the red mode to green.

In the reactive state of our brain, we are disoriented and don't see development opportunities.

Your brain strives to be responsive because it functions well that way. This state contributes to your well-being and physical and mental health and is the basis of longevity.

Did you know? 10% of people don't know how they feel. It is called alexithymia. Because of this dysfunction, a person can't describe their emotions in words, distinguish one feeling from another, or understand the mood of others.

chapters.title: Accept the good to anchor the positive experience
chapters.text:
The HEAL method means:

• H — Have a positive experience.
• E — Enrich it.
• A — Absorb it.
• L — Link positive and negative material.

The first two steps involve increasing positive experiences and effectively assimilating them. The last step guarantees that we remember joyful experiences and can put them into practice. We often have small valuable daily occurrences, like pleasant small talk or tasteful food, although we are often unaware. It takes learning and practice to saturate our brains with these experiences. If you have six brief, pleasant experiences in a day and choose to savor them, they will add to something big for you. You can savor these experiences at the end of the day or throughout the day.

These seemingly ordinary experiences you create have hidden power. They can build resilience, heal distress and dysfunction, improve relationships, promote physical health, and grow durable happiness.

Some of these positive experiences already exist. It would be best if you simply notice them. Others are in the background of your awareness, and you need to bring them to the foreground, where you can catch and enrich them.

It is critical to be aware of thoughts that positive experiences trigger. What images, perspectives, expectations, plans, and memories does this experience bring you? Also, a positive experience can contain a sense perception, an emotion, a desire, or an action. Sensory impressions include feelings of joy, relaxation, vitality, and strength. Desires relate to your hopes, morals, intentions, and wants, while actions speak to your inclinations and skills.

The ability to activate inner strength based on positive experiences is crucial to managing daily tasks, interacting effectively with the surrounding world, and being productive.

The more you refer to good and pleasant memories, the faster they become part of your inner positive force.

You can create positive experiences based on small pleasant moments. It can be anything in your present or past life. For example, personal qualities you possess, the lives of others, imagining the future, finding the good in the bad, imagining things that can be and can’t be, and caring for others. When we perceive life events not as obstacles but as opportunities, it is also an example of a good moment.

chapters.title: Over time, positive experiences supersede negative ones
chapters.text:
Not only can unpleasant experiences not be avoided entirely, but they have value. They teach and warn us. Perceiving it this way, we endow a negative experience with positive features.

Negative memories usually appear unexpectedly under the influence of certain external factors. For example, you accidentally met a colleague from a past job and remembered how much you didn't like your previous position and how much time you spent at work without rest. You feel a surge of unpleasant experiences again. After we calm down and distract ourselves with something else, these memories are re-fixed in the depths of memory.

Currently, we can dilute unpleasant and disturbing thoughts with positive ones, neutralizing the impact of the former, before they have time to re-record in memory. For example, in a situation with unpleasant memories of past work, you can remember good moments such as fun corporate events or a friendly team. Of course, talking about traumatic situations when looking for the positive is inappropriate and impossible. But we can rewrite most of the unpleasant life events in memory. As a result, the next time you remember an uncomfortable or disturbing situation, it will no longer have the power to cause strong negative emotions.

Good experience is an antidote for harmful material.

When you experience an unpleasant situation, don't try to forget about it as soon as possible. Empathize and support yourself. Only after you feel relief, find some positive moments.

Once you can associate positive experiences with toxic materials, you will learn to cope better with difficult situations.

Psychotherapy, mindfulness training, and meditation can help accept good moments more easily. When faced with an unpleasant experience again, rely on the good things that have already happened. These memories can be your support.

Finding the good and accepting it can be challenging. Sometimes we face inner resistance and reluctance to notice the positive. This resistance is helpful because it allows us to learn more about ourselves. Explore your feelings and understand what prevents you from seeing the good. It may be a potent offense or anger. Give yourself time to experience difficult moments, and only then try to look at situations from the other side.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Striving to react responsively is crucial not only for each individual but also for society as a whole. When our brain works in reactive mode, we cannot worry about the environment in which we live. We cut down forests and use other natural resources without limits. Feelings of lack and anxiety force us to consume incessantly and uncontrollably. However, the conditions of our lives today fundamentally differ from those in which our ancestors existed. They were unable to provide their basic needs stably and dependably.

By realizing that our basic needs are already met or do not require much effort, we can move from merely consuming to giving and helping others.

We must look for contentment, security, and belonging within ourselves, not the outside. We strengthen our inner power by giving thanks for what we already have, relaxing, empathizing, and caring. Look for the good in every moment of life to build internal support and be a source of positivity for yourself.

Try this:


1. Before going to sleep, feel gratitude for the day you have lived and wish yourself love and happiness. It will help you focus on the good and find inner harmony constantly.
2. Practice paying attention to your feelings. The food you taste may be delicious, or the coffee may be incredibly aromatic.
3. Observe what surrounds you. For example, carefully examine the trees and their leaves while walking in the park. What color and shape are they? Learning to notice what's around you helps you develop an awareness of your feelings and emotions.

Read it till the end?
